Acedia: From Greek akēdia (ἀκηδία), "carelessness" or "listlessness." One of the eight primary passions in Orthodox ascetic theology (following Evagrius of Pontus and John Cassian), sometimes translated as "sloth" but more precisely as spiritual despondency or torpor — the "noonday demon" (Psalm 91:6 LXX). Acedia is the interior darkening that causes the soul to lose its taste for prayer, withdraw from ascetic effort, and become numb to spiritual realities — often disguised as boredom, restlessness, or the sense that nothing matters. The desert fathers regarded it as one of the most dangerous passions precisely because it does not feel like a passion from the inside; it presents as a reasonable assessment of meaninglessness or futility. The remedy prescribed in the tradition is patient perseverance in one's rule of prayer and life, refusing to act on the logismoi of hopelessness, and the practice of compunction — allowing the grief for one's condition to become the energy of repentance rather than the weight of despair.
Antidoron: From Greek antidoron (ἀντίδωρον), "instead of the gift" — from anti- (instead of) + doron (gift). Blessed (but not consecrated) bread distributed to all present at the end of the Divine Liturgy, Orthodox and non-Orthodox alike. The antidoron is a sign of fellowship and hospitality: the Church's way of saying "you are not a stranger here" to those who cannot yet receive the Eucharist (visitors, catechumens, those who did not prepare with fasting and confession). Historically it derives from the portions of the prosphora loaf not used for the Lamb (the Eucharistic bread); it is blessed, not transfigured, and carries no Eucharistic theology of Real Presence. For the inquirer, receiving the antidoron is often the first physical participation in the liturgical life.
Anamnesis: From Greek anamnēsis (ἀνάμνησις), "remembrance" or "memorial." In the Eucharistic liturgy, anamnesis is the prayer that follows the Words of Institution ("Do this in remembrance of me"), in which the Church declares that it is making present — not merely recalling — the death, resurrection, and ascension of Christ. The Orthodox understanding of anamnesis is stronger than the English word "memory" implies: it is a liturgical re-presentation of Christ's one unrepeatable sacrifice, not a psychological act of remembrance. The Eucharistic anamnesis is why the Divine Liturgy is not a "new" sacrifice — it is the single sacrifice of Calvary perpetually accessible to the Church through the liturgical action. This distinguishes the Orthodox and Catholic understanding of the Eucharist from the Zwinglian symbolic memorial view.
Anapausis: From Greek anapausis (ἀνάπαυσις), "rest," "repose," or "refreshment" — from ana- (again, up) + pauō (to cease, to give rest). In Orthodox ascetic and liturgical theology, anapausis is not idleness or mere cessation of labor but the active repose of the soul in God — the interior stillness of the nous gathered into the heart and at rest in its Maker. It is the word Christ uses in Matthew 11:28-29: "I will give you rest (ἀνάπαυσιν)... and you will find rest for your souls." The Fathers read this rest as the fruit of laying down the heavy yoke of self-will and the passions, especially merimna (anxious self-management); the soul that stops striving to carry its own life finds the repose that only Christ's easy yoke can give. Anapausis is closely allied with hesychia (stillness) and belongs to the movement of katharsis toward theoria: the burdened, scattered soul is brought to a settled peace in which unceasing prayer becomes possible. The term also names the "place of repose" prayed for the departed in Orthodox funeral and memorial services ("...in a place of light, a place of anapausis").
Anaphora: From Greek anaphora (ἀναφορά), "offering" or "carrying up." The central Eucharistic prayer of the Divine Liturgy — the great prayer of offering that includes the narrative of institution, the anamnesis (memorial), the epiclesis (invocation of the Holy Spirit), and the intercessions. In Orthodox theology, the entire anaphora — not any single moment within it — is the consecratory prayer through which the bread and wine are offered and become the Body and Blood of Christ. The word's root (ana- "up" + pherō "to carry/bear") names the movement of the prayer: the Church's offering is carried up to God while the grace of the Holy Spirit descends. John 17 has been read by patristic interpreters as the theological prototype of the High Priestly prayer structure that the anaphora enacts — Christ offering Himself to the Father while interceding for those given to Him.
Anthropocentric: Placing the human person at the center of meaning, value, or cosmology. In theology, an anthropocentric approach interprets religious reality primarily in terms of human needs, experience, or reason rather than beginning from God. Orthodox theology is theocentric (God-centered) and views anthropocentrism as a distortion of the proper ordering of creation — one that ultimately diminishes the human person by severing it from its divine source and end.
Apatheia: The state of interior stillness in which the passions (πάθη) no longer dominate or distort the soul's relationship with God — not the absence of feeling but the ordering and healing of the emotional life so that the nous is free to attend to God. In Orthodox ascetic theology, apatheia is the fruit of katharsis (purification) and the threshold of theoria (contemplation): the soul that has been freed from the tyranny of logismoi can perceive the uncreated divine light. It is a gift of grace, not an achievement, and requires ongoing watchfulness (nepsis) to sustain.
Apokatastasis: From Greek ἀποκατάστασις (apokatastasis), "restoration" or "reestablishment" (the word appears in Acts 3:21, "the restoration of all things"). As a technical theological term it names the doctrine — associated especially with Origen and condemned under the Fifth Ecumenical Council (Constantinople II, 553) — that hell is not eternal and that in the end all rational beings, even the demons, are finally saved and restored to God. Orthodoxy affirms the real and lasting reality of hell as freely chosen, and so rejects apokatastasis as a required belief. The term matters pastorally because certain Fathers (notably St. Isaac the Syrian and St. Gregory of Nyssa) speak with such hope of the boundless love of God that their words are sometimes stretched into universalism; the Church reads them within the boundary set by the councils — God's love reaches even the lost, but it does not override the freedom of the heart that refuses Him.
Archiereus: From Greek ἀρχιερεύς (archiereus), "high priest" — literally, "chief priest" (archi- = chief, hiereus = priest). In Orthodox theology and liturgical usage, Archiereus refers above all to Christ as the Great High Priest, the title Hebrews develops at length (Heb 4:14-10:18): the one who is holy, innocent, undefiled, and who ministers in the true heavenly sanctuary before the Father. The Divine Liturgy enacts what Hebrews describes — the earthly offering participates in the perpetual heavenly ministry of Christ the Archiereus. In ecclesiastical usage, the term also designates a bishop (ἀρχιερεύς is the Greek title for a bishop, reflecting the bishop's role as the earthly image of the Great High Priest). Patristic theology uses the title to ground the Christian's parrhesia (bold confidence) in prayer: because our Archiereus is sinless and his intercession is perfect, we can approach the throne of grace with compunction rather than paralytic shame.
Arianism: The Christological heresy propagated by Arius of Alexandria (c. 256–336 AD), teaching that the Son of God was not coeternal or coequal with the Father but was a created being — the highest of all creatures, existing before the material world, but with a beginning in time. The Arian formula was: ēn pote hote ouk ēn — "there was a time when He was not." Arius grounded his position in scripture: Prov 8:22 ("The Lord created me at the beginning of His works"), John 14:28 ("The Father is greater than I"), and Col 1:15 ("firstborn of all creation"). His teaching spread rapidly across the Roman Empire in the early 4th century — popular enough that theological debates broke out in markets and bakeries. The Council of Nicaea (325 AD) condemned Arianism and defined the Son as homoousios ("of the same substance") with the Father. The Arian controversy did not end at Nicaea; the Arian party nearly reversed the decision for the next 60 years, winning the support of Emperors Constantine (late in his reign) and Constantius. The Nicene faith was permanently reaffirmed only at the Council of Constantinople (381 AD). Arianism anticipates the Christology of modern groups such as Jehovah's Witnesses. The theological answer to Arianism remains the key reason the Nicene Creed was necessary: scripture alone proved insufficient to adjudicate the debate.
Athanasius Contra Mundum: Latin: "Athanasius against the world." The historical phrase applied to St. Athanasius the Great (c. 296–373 AD), Bishop of Alexandria, who defended the Nicene faith — the full divinity of the Son (homoousios, "of the same substance" as the Father) — during the decades when Arianism held political and ecclesiastical dominance throughout the Roman Empire. Athanasius was exiled five times by four different Roman emperors for refusing to compromise the Nicene definition. He spent 17 of his 45 years as bishop driven from his see, hunted by imperial soldiers, hiding among desert monks. His writings were banned and his name slandered. The phrase captures the paradox at the center of the Trinitarian controversy: truth in the Church is not determined by institutional majorities or imperial favor, but by fidelity to the apostolic deposit. When the whole empire had seemingly gone Arian — emperors, bishops, and councils included — Athanasius held the line alone. His feast day is May 2 (shared with Pope Cyril of Alexandria, his successor in defending the Council of Ephesus). The phrase is used more broadly in Orthodox theology and apologetics to name the pattern of faithful minority witness against dominant error — and as a caution that the Church's truth is never to be equated with whatever holds ecclesiastical power at a given moment. See [[source_uoc_09_trinity_nicaea_325]].
Athymia: From Greek ἀθυμία (despondency, low-spiritedness, loss of heart). A specific form of interior darkness in Orthodox ascetic theology — distinguished from acedia (general spiritual torpor) by its more acute character: the logismos that reads prolonged suffering or divine silence as evidence of God's abandonment or personal condemnation. Where acedia is a slow numbing, athymia is a sharper interior collapse — the soul losing its courage to continue in prayer. The desert fathers prescribed hypomonē (patient endurance) as the remedy: continuing in one's prayer rule not because consolation is present but because the act of perseverance in the dark is itself the purification of trust.
Catechesis: From Greek katēchēsis (oral instruction). The process of systematic instruction in the Christian faith, preparing individuals for baptism and full participation in the life of the Church. In Orthodox practice, catechesis typically extends over months or years, covering the Creed, the sacraments, Holy Scripture, the Church Fathers, and the liturgical life. The catechumen is not merely informed but formed — gradually shaped into the life of the Body of Christ.
Chrismated: Past participle of chrismate. Having received the sacrament of Chrismation — anointed with holy chrism as part of sacramental initiation into the Orthodox Church. A chrismated Orthodox Christian has received the seal of the gift of the Holy Spirit and is fully united to the Body of Christ.
Chrismation: From Greek chrisma (anointing). The sacrament (Holy Mystery) in which a newly baptized person is anointed with holy oil (chrism) by the priest, conferring the gifts of the Holy Spirit. In Orthodox practice, Chrismation immediately follows Baptism and precedes first Holy Communion, completing the three sacraments of initiation. The anointing consecrates the whole person — forehead, eyes, nostrils, lips, ears, breast, hands, and feet — as a living temple of the Holy Spirit. Equivalent to Western Confirmation, though administered at baptism regardless of age.
Compunction: From Greek katanyxis (κατάνυξις), "piercing" or "pricking." The spiritual experience of being broken open before God — a deep grief and sorrow for one's sins combined with a movement of hope and longing for God's mercy. Compunction is not mere psychological guilt but a gift of grace: the soul pierced by awareness of its distance from God, and thereby awakened to a deeper desire for Him. The desert fathers and the Philokalia tradition treat compunction as the essential fuel of continual prayer — tears of compunction (penthos) are the sign that the nous has genuinely encountered its condition before God. Compunction belongs to the katharsis stage of the spiritual life and is the interior soil in which humility grows.
Consecration: The act by which bread and wine are set apart and transformed into the Body and Blood of Christ in the Divine Liturgy. In Orthodox theology, consecration occurs through the entire Eucharistic prayer (anaphora) and the invocation of the Holy Spirit (epiclesis), not at a single isolated moment. The term also applies to the setting apart of persons (bishops, priests) or objects (churches, altars, holy water) for sacred use through prayer and anointing.
Consubstantial: From Latin consubstantialis; Greek equivalent: homoousios (ὁμοούσιος). "Of the same substance" or "of one being." The Nicene Creed's defining term for the Son's relationship to the Father: the Son is consubstantial with the Father — not merely similar in nature but identical in divine essence. The term was the decisive weapon of the Council of Nicaea (325) against Arianism, which held the Son to be a created being — highest of creatures, but not God by nature. Against this, Nicaea declared the Son homoousios with the Father: fully and truly God. The same term applies to the Holy Spirit in the expanded Niceno-Constantinopolitan Creed (381). "Consubstantial with the Father" replaces the earlier English rendering "of one being with the Father" in the 2011 Roman Rite revision; the Orthodox liturgical tradition has always preserved the Greek homoousios.
Deilia: From Greek deilia (δειλία), "cowardice" or "fearfulness." In Orthodox ascetic theology, deilia is numbered among the passions (πάθη) — specifically the disordered fear that prevents the soul from bearing witness, enduring trial, or acting from conscience before God. Distinguished from prudent caution, deilia is the inordinate attachment to one's own safety, reputation, or comfort that overrides fidelity to truth and to God. The desert fathers and Chrysostom identify deilia as the interior counterpart to vainglory (κενοδοξία): where vainglory craves human approval, deilia shrinks from human disapproval — both passions reveal the same root disorder, a gaze anchored to human opinion rather than to God. The remedy is παρρησία (parrhesia) — the holy boldness before God and men that comes from a conscience formed by the Holy Spirit and no longer dependent on exterior approval.
Dianoia: From Greek dianoia (διάνοια), "discursive reasoning" or "rational thought." In Orthodox anthropology, dianoia is the analytical, conceptual faculty of the mind — the capacity for stepwise reasoning, comparison, and propositional thought. It is distinct from and lower than the nous (νοῦς), which is the soul's faculty for direct perception of God. The two are not opposed but ordered: dianoia is necessary and good in its own sphere (theology, articulation, distinction-making), but it cannot by itself yield knowledge of God. The fallen condition is in part the inversion of this order — dianoia tries to operate in the place of the nous, reducing God to an object of analysis. The healing of the spiritual life involves the gradual purification and gathering of the nous so that it can perceive what dianoia can only describe; theoria belongs to the awakened nous, not to dianoia.
Diakrisis: From Greek diakrisis (διάκρισις), "discernment" or "distinguishing of spirits." In Orthodox ascetic theology, diakrisis is the capacity to distinguish the true movements of the Holy Spirit from counterfeit impulses — whether arising from the self, the world, or demonic influence. The desert tradition, particularly through the Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum) and Evagrius of Pontus, regards diakrisis as the "queen of virtues," because without it the soul cannot safely navigate the spiritual life: asceticism without diakrisis leads to prelest (spiritual delusion), charity without diakrisis to manipulation, and prayer without diakrisis to fantasy. The Philokalia tradition closely connects diakrisis with nepsis (watchfulness) — one cannot discern what one is not first attentively watching. John Climacus teaches that diakrisis matures only through humility: the soul that knows itself honestly before God gains the clarity needed to distinguish genuine grace from counterfeit consolation.
Didache: From Greek didache (διδαχή), "teaching" or "instruction." The Didache, fully titled "The Teaching of the Twelve Apostles," is one of the oldest Christian documents outside the New Testament, dated by scholars as early as 70 AD — possibly contemporary with some NT books. Rediscovered in 1873 in a Constantinople library by Metropolitan Philotheos Bryennios. It prescribes Trinitarian baptism by immersion, fasting on Wednesdays and Fridays, the Lord's Prayer three times daily, the appointment of bishops and deacons, and formal Eucharistic prayers calling the Eucharist a sacrifice and restricting communion to the baptized. The Didache is a primary witness against the claim that early Christian worship was spontaneous and unstructured — it describes liturgical, sacramental, hierarchical worship from the apostolic generation itself.
Docetism: From Greek dokein (δοκεῖν) — "to seem" or "to appear." The first major Christological heresy confronted by the Church, denying the reality of Christ's human body, suffering, and death. Docetists held that the Son of God only appeared to become flesh, appeared to suffer, and appeared to die — the material world being considered unworthy of or incompatible with divinity. The Fathers (especially Chrysostom and Ignatius of Antioch) understood the evangelists' inclusion of Christ's fear and agony in Gethsemane as deliberate anti-Docetist testimony: a merely apparent body neither sweats blood nor needs angelic strengthening. Docetism's underlying logic — that God cannot genuinely enter creaturely vulnerability — remains the most persistent implicit heresy in popular piety even today, surfacing whenever Christ's human suffering is sentimentalized or minimized.
Dyothelitism: From Greek dyo (two) + thelēma (θέλημα — will). The Orthodox doctrine, defined at the Third Council of Constantinople (681 AD), that Christ possesses two wills — one divine and one human — with his human will always freely submitting to the divine. The opposing heresy, Monothelitism (one will in Christ), was condemned as heretical because it effectively absorbed the human will into the divine and left no room for a genuine human act of obedience. The Gethsemane prayer ("not my will but thine") is the dyothelite text par excellence: it presupposes two genuinely distinct wills in the one Person of the Son. The Passion has no salvific force without a real human act of freedom — Christ's free submission of his human will to the Father is the interior act that gives the Crucifixion its redemptive character. John of Damascus and Maximus the Confessor are the primary Patristic defenders of this doctrine.
Energeia: From Greek energeia (ἐνέργεια), "activity," "operation," or "working" (plural energeiai) — from en- (in) + ergon (work). In Orthodox theology, the divine energies are God's uncreated operations and self-manifestations — His love, light, glory, grace, and power — by which He goes out from Himself toward creation and is genuinely known, encountered, and participated in. They are distinguished (but not separated) from the divine essence (ousia), which remains forever transcendent and unknowable. This essence-energies distinction, rooted in St. Basil's Letter 234 ("we know God from His energies, but we do not approach His essence") and the Cappadocian Fathers, was systematized by St. Gregory Palamas (14th c.) against Barlaam of Calabria. It is the dogmatic safeguard of theosis: the deified creature participates really in God's uncreated energies — and so in God Himself — without ever touching or becoming the divine essence. Hence "partakers of the divine nature" (2 Peter 1:4) is union, not pantheism. The New Testament uses energeia of God's active working in believers (Ephesians 1:19, 3:7; Colossians 1:29). See [[concept_palamism_and_divine_energies]] and [[concept_theosis]].
Epiclesis: From Greek epiklēsis (ἐπίκλησις), "invocation" or "calling upon" — from epi- (upon) + kaleō (to call). The prayer within the Eucharistic anaphora in which the celebrant explicitly invokes the Holy Spirit to descend upon the bread and wine and upon the worshipping assembly. In Orthodox theology, the epiclesis is the moment within the broader anaphoric prayer at which the transformation of the gifts is understood to be effected: the priest prays, "Send down Your Holy Spirit upon us and upon these gifts here offered," naming the Spirit as the agent of consecration. This distinguishes Orthodox Eucharistic theology from the Western (Roman Rite) emphasis on the Words of Institution as the moment of consecration. The epiclesis thus enacts theologically what John 17:22 prays: the Holy Spirit — the divine glory given by Christ to His disciples — is called down to dwell in the assembly and in the Eucharistic gifts, extending the perichoretic life of the Trinity into the worshipping community.
Epithumētikon: From Greek epithumētikon (ἐπιθυμητικόν), "the desiderative faculty" or "the appetitive part" — from epithumia (ἐπιθυμία, desire, longing). In the patristic tripartite anthropology of the soul, the epithumētikon is the faculty of desire: the soul's capacity to long for, reach toward, and attach itself to objects. In its ordered state, it directs the soul's desire toward God and toward good things in their proper measure. In its disordered state, it becomes the seat of the passions of lust, gluttony, and avarice — the logismoi that arise when desire latches onto created goods as ultimate ends (the dynamic Paul diagnoses in Romans 1:25). The healing of the epithumētikon is a central work of katharsis: fasting, the Jesus Prayer, and the redirection of desire toward God gradually order the appetitive faculty so that it desires what it was made to desire — the divine goodness itself. See also: thumikon (irascible faculty), logistikon / nous (rational faculty).
Ekstasis: From Greek ἔκστασις (standing outside oneself; transport, amazement, ecstasy). In ordinary Greek, a state of being displaced from one's normal condition — astonishment, confusion, or trance. In the LXX and patristic theology, ekstasis carries layered meanings: (1) the soul's movement outside itself in response to the overwhelming reality of God, as when the soul perceives divine beauty beyond what it can contain; (2) the holy disorientation of one who, under extreme suffering, discovers that divine mercy exceeds even the capacity of despair; and (3) in the LXX superscription of Psalm 30, a prophetic or visionary state in which David composes the Psalm. The hesychast tradition is cautious about ekstasis as a goal — genuine ekstasis is given by grace, not cultivated as an experience, and must be distinguished from diabolical imitation. The word appears twice in Psalm 30 (LXX): in the superscription and at v.22, where the psalmist says "I said in my ekstasis, I am cast away from before Your eyes" — naming the moment of perceived abandonment as itself a kind of holy transport.
Eucharistia: From Greek eucharistia (εὐχαριστία), "thanksgiving." The word from which the English term "Eucharist" derives. In the New Testament, it appears as ordinary thanksgiving (e.g., 1 Corinthians 14:16) but also in distinctly Eucharistic contexts — most significantly Hebrews 13:15 ("let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise [θυσίαν αἰνέσεως] to God — that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name"), where eucharistia describes the verbal thanksgiving component of the full Eucharistic offering. In Orthodox liturgical theology, the entire Eucharistic celebration is an act of eucharistia — thanksgiving for creation, redemption, and deification. The use of the word in Hebrews 13:15, in context of an altar from which Levitical priests "have no right to eat" (v.10), is a patristic and Orthodox argument that the passage describes the Christian Eucharistic rite as distinct from and superior to the OT animal sacrifice system.
Exaltation: The raising to a position of supreme honor, glory, or dignity. In Orthodox Christology, the exaltation of Christ refers to His resurrection, ascension, and glorification at the right hand of the Father — the counterpart to His self-emptying (kenosis) in the Incarnation and Passion. Philippians 2:9: "Therefore God has highly exalted Him and given Him the name above every name." The term also designates the feast of the Exaltation of the Holy Cross (September 14), commemorating the finding and elevation of the True Cross by St. Helena.
Exalted: Past participle and adjective of exalt. Raised to the highest honor, glory, or dignity. In Christology, Christ is exalted at the Resurrection and Ascension, declared Lord of all. In liturgical language, used of the Cross at the feast of the Exaltation — physically lifted high before the assembly as a proclamation of its victory.
Exomologesis: From Greek exomologēsis (ἐξομολόγησις), "full confession" or "complete acknowledgment" — from ek- (out, completely) + homologein (to acknowledge, to speak the same word as). In Orthodox liturgical and ascetic theology, exomologesis is the act of confessing sin openly before God, naming what was hidden and bringing it into the light of divine mercy. The term appears in the LXX of Psalm 31:5 (ἐξομολογήσομαι — "I will confess/acknowledge my transgression to the Lord"), which the Fathers regard as the scriptural warrant for the Sacrament of Holy Confession. The prefix ek- is significant: exomologesis is not interior acknowledgment alone but an outward, verbal act — the soul bringing completely out what it had suppressed within. This externalizing quality is why the Orthodox tradition insists that sacramental confession is spoken aloud to a priest, not merely resolved inwardly: the act of naming sin before a witness completes the movement that silent groaning (Psalm 31:3-4) could not. The broader Greek-patristic usage of exomologēsis also carries the sense of thanksgiving or doxology — genuine confession of sin turns, in the Fathers, into gratitude for mercy received, so that the same act that names the soul's poverty becomes praise for God's abundance.
Filioque: Latin: filioque, "and the Son." The clause inserted into the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed (381 AD) by the Western Church, so that the procession of the Holy Spirit reads "who proceeds from the Father and the Son" instead of the original "who proceeds from the Father." The addition first appeared locally in Visigothic Spain (6th c.), spread through the Frankish Empire, and was officially adopted by Rome over the following centuries. The Orthodox Church rejects the Filioque on two grounds: (1) theologically, it confuses the monarchy of the Father — the Father alone is the unoriginate source of both Son and Spirit, and dual-procession blurs the Trinitarian relations the Cappadocian Fathers articulated; (2) canonically, an ecumenical creed cannot be altered without an ecumenical council, and no such council authorized the addition. The Filioque controversy is one of the theological faults behind the Great Schism of 1054. See [[concept_palamism_and_divine_energies]] (Monarchical Trinitarianism).
Hematohidrosis: Also spelled hematidrosis or hemidrosis — from Greek haima (αἷμα — blood) + hidrōsis (ἵδρωσις — sweating). A rare but clinically documented medical condition in which extreme psychological or physical stress causes the capillary blood vessels surrounding the sweat glands to rupture, releasing blood into and through the sweat ducts. Documented cases are associated with "acute fear or intense mental contemplation" under life-threatening conditions. The physiological mechanism involves a prolonged sympathetic fight-or-flight response (blood driven away from the skin to the muscles) followed by a parasympathetic recovery phase (blood returning to the skin with sufficient pressure to rupture the capillaries intertwined with the sweat glands). Luke 22:44 — "his sweat became like great drops (thromboi) of blood falling down upon the ground" — is understood in Orthodox theology as a clinically coherent description of hematohidrosis, testifying to the maximum degree of anticipatory suffering Christ endured in the garden before any physical violence had begun. Its theological significance is anti-Docetist: a merely apparent body does not bleed through pores.
Hesychia: From Greek hēsychia (ἡσυχία), "stillness," "silence," "rest." The state of interior quiet and gathered attention that is both the goal and the practice of the Orthodox hesychast tradition. Hesychia does not mean the absence of external noise or even the absence of thought, but the gathered stillness of the nous in God — a quality of interior orientation that can be maintained even amid external turbulence. The hesychast tradition (centered in monasticism and the Philokalia, especially Gregory Palamas's 14th-century defense) holds that hesychia is the necessary condition for unceasing prayer: without interior stillness, the nous is too scattered to pray from the heart rather than from the surface of the mind. St. John of the Ladder describes hesychia as "the laying aside of thoughts" and "the guardian of the nous." The Jesus Prayer is the primary instrument of hesychia in the active Christian life, gradually training the nous to remain present to God rather than dispersed among the logismoi.
Hodegetria: From Greek Hodēgētria (Ὁδηγήτρια), "She Who Shows the Way" — from hodēgos (ὁδηγός), "guide" or "leader." The most theologically significant of the major Theotokos icon types. In the Hodegetria, Mary holds the Christ Child on one arm and gestures toward Him with the other hand, directing the viewer's gaze from herself to her Son. The icon is the visual embodiment of the Orthodox Marian principle: Mary does not draw attention to herself but points to Christ. Tradition traces the original Hodegetria to St. Luke the Evangelist; the most famous copy was the Hodegetria of Constantinople (destroyed in 1453). Other major Theotokos icon types include the Eleousa (Tenderness, where Mary and Christ press cheeks) and the Platytera ("More Spacious Than the Heavens," where Mary's womb contains the uncontainable Logos). In all types, Mary is almost never depicted alone — she is always presented in relation to Christ, the theological content she bears.
Hypakoē: From Greek hypakoē (ὑπακοή), "obedience," "hearing," or "attentive listening." A virtue in Orthodox ascetic theology that names the active, trained capacity to hear and respond to God — particularly the Shepherd's voice amid the competing logismoi. Hypakoē is not passive submission but a cultivated interior attentiveness: the sheep who "hear the Shepherd's voice" (John 10:27) do so not by accident but through practiced nepsis. The term carries both the sense of listening (akouō — to hear) and obedience (hypo — under), suggesting that genuine hearing of God's voice necessarily issues in following. In the ascetic tradition, hypakoē is the virtue directly opposed to the logismos of spiritual distraction: the soul that practices the Jesus Prayer is training precisely this capacity — to attend to the Shepherd's call above all competing inner voices. The Philokalia writers (Hesychios, Nikephoros, Gregory of Sinai) present this cultivated listening as a central goal of hesychast practice.
Hypomonē: From Greek hypomonē (ὑπομονή), "patient endurance" or "steadfast perseverance." A cardinal virtue in Orthodox ascetic theology, distinct from mere passive suffering. Hypomonē is the active, watchful quality of remaining present to God's will through difficulty without complaint, flight, or capitulation to the passions. St. Paul identifies it as the fruit of tribulation (Romans 5:3-5), and Acts 14:22 presents it as normative for the Christian life: "through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God." The Fathers distinguish hypomonē from simple stubbornness or stoic endurance: genuine hypomonē is animated by love and hope, directed toward God, and inseparable from prayer. In the journey of katharsis, tribulations borne in hypomonē become instruments of purification — the resistance that strengthens the nous rather than breaks it. The Philokalia tradition connects hypomonē closely with nepsis: to endure well one must remain watchfully present, neither escaping into fantasy nor collapsing into despair.
Hypostasis: From Greek hypostasis (ὑπόστασις), literally "that which stands under." In Orthodox Trinitarian and Christological theology, hypostasis names the concrete, particular Person — that which exists in a distinct, unrepeatable mode — as distinguished from ousia (essence or nature), which names what something is in its general kind. The Cappadocian Fathers (especially St. Basil and St. Gregory of Nyssa) crystallized the Trinitarian formula: God is one ousia in three hypostases — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — sharing a single divine nature while existing as three distinct Persons in genuine relation. In Christology, the Council of Chalcedon (451) confessed that the divine and human natures of Christ are united "without confusion, without change, without division, without separation" in the single hypostasis of the eternal Son — the hypostatic union. The term safeguards both the unity of God and the genuine personal distinction within God, refusing both modalism (where the three Persons collapse into one) and tritheism (where the three become three gods).
Hyiothesia: From Greek hyiothesia (υἱοθεσία), literally "son-placing" — the act of being placed as a son. In Orthodox theology, hyiothesia (often rendered "divine adoption" or "adoptive sonship") names the grace by which human beings enter, through union with the eternal Son, into the Son's own filial relationship with the Father. It is not a legal fiction (as in some Western forensic accounts) but an ontological participation: through Baptism and the Holy Spirit, the soul genuinely shares in the Son's mode of existence as beloved child of the Father. St. Cyril of Alexandria draws on hyiothesia to explain what it means to pray "in the name of Jesus" — the soul praying in the Son's name is already inside the divine love it is asking for, because adoption has made the Father's love its own. Hyiothesia is the anthropological ground of theosis: the soul does not approach God as a stranger petitioning an alien deity but as a child returning to the Father's house.
Kardia: From Greek kardia (καρδία), "heart." In Orthodox anthropology and ascetic theology, the kardia is not merely the physical organ but the spiritual center of the human person — the innermost depth where the nous (spiritual intellect) and will converge, and where the human person's encounter with God most properly occurs. The Fathers consistently distinguish between surface-level rational activity (dianoia) and the deeper place of the kardia: "The Lord does not look at outward appearance, but at the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7). The goal of hesychasm and the Jesus Prayer is the "descent of the nous into the heart" — the gathering of scattered mental activity back into the kardia, where unceasing prayer is possible. Psalm 32 (LXX) v. 15 — "He fashions the hearts of them all individually" (κατὰ μόνας) — is the Psalter's clearest witness to the kardia as uniquely known and uniquely made by God: each person's kardia is not an instance of a type but a singular creature, fashioned and seen by its Maker. The corruption of the kardia by the passions is the root spiritual problem; its purification through katharsis and its illumination through the Jesus Prayer is the substance of the spiritual life.
Katharsis: From Greek katharsis (κάθαρσις), "purification" or "cleansing." In Orthodox spiritual theology, katharsis is the first of three stages of the spiritual life: purification, illumination (photismos), and union (theosis). It consists in the purification of the passions (πάθη) through ascetic struggle, repentance, and the grace of the sacraments. The soul in katharsis works to detach from disordered desires, cultivate virtues, and clear the nous from the logismoi that cloud its perception of God. Katharsis is not self-improvement but cooperation with divine grace in the reordering of the whole person — body and soul together — toward God.
Katholike Ekklesia: From Greek he katholike ekklesia (ἡ καθολικὴ ἐκκλησία), "the Catholic Church" — literally, "the whole/universal assembly." The phrase first appears in writing in Ignatius of Antioch's Letter to the Smyrnaeans (c. 107 AD): "Wherever the bishop appears, let the people be there, just as wherever Jesus Christ is, there is the Catholic Church." Ignatius used the term not to designate a Roman institution (he was bishop of Antioch, not Rome) but to distinguish the one universal Church — gathered around its bishop and the Eucharist — from heretical splinter groups. The word katholikos (from kata + holos, "according to the whole") means universal, complete, lacking nothing. The term predates both the Roman Catholic Church and the Great Schism by nearly a millennium. When the Nicene Creed confesses "one holy catholic and apostolic Church," it uses this word in its original Ignatian sense.
Kecharitomene: Greek: kecharitomene. "Full of grace" or "the one who has been graced." The angelic greeting to Mary in Luke 1:28. A perfect passive participle indicating a completed and enduring state of divine grace bestowed upon her.
Kenosis: Greek: kenosis. "Self-emptying." The voluntary self-emptying of Christ, who, being God, took on human nature and the form of a servant (Philippians 2:5–8). A central concept in Orthodox Christology.
Kenotic: The adjective form of kenosis; describing anything relating to Christ's self-emptying of divine prerogatives in the Incarnation.
Komboskini: From Greek κομβοσκοίνι (komboskini), the Orthodox prayer rope (Russian: chotki, vervitsa) — a loop of wool knotted by a monastic, typically of 33, 100, or 300 knots, used to count repetitions of the Jesus Prayer. Each knot marks one prayer: the one praying holds a knot, says "Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me, a sinner," and moves to the next. Traditionally tied with knots each formed of many interwoven crosses (so that the demons "cannot untie" them), the komboskini is given at monastic tonsure and is also used by laypeople. Its purpose is not magical counting but the gathering of a scattered mind into rhythmic, bodily prayer — an aid toward unceasing prayer (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and the descent of the prayer "from the mouth to the mind to the heart" (see: [[Prayer of the Heart]], Hesychia).
Latria: From Greek latreia (λατρεία), "worship," "service," or "adoration" — cognate with latreuō (to serve, to worship). In Orthodox theology, latria designates the absolute worship due to God alone: adoration, sacrifice, and the full surrender of the self to the divine majesty. It is rigorously distinguished from proskynesis (veneration/reverence) — the honor appropriate to holy persons, icons, relics, and the Theotokos — and from douleia (service or honor rendered to fellow creatures). The distinction is not merely semantic; it is the theological foundation of the Orthodox defense of iconography. The Seventh Ecumenical Council (Nicaea II, 787) formalized this distinction: the Church offers latria to God alone, while directing reverent proskynesis through icons and saints toward God as their source and end. The veneration given to an icon passes through to the prototype (the person depicted); since the prototype is a creature — even a glorified saint — the act is proskynesis, not latria. This is why Orthodox veneration of the Theotokos, the saints, and icons is not idolatry: idolatry is the disordering of latria toward a creature, which is precisely what Orthodox theology refuses.
Logismoi: Greek plural of logismos (λογισμός), "thought" or "reasoning." In Orthodox ascetic theology, the logismoi are the stream of thoughts — including tempting, distracting, and disordered thoughts — that continually arise in the mind and heart. The desert fathers identified eight primary logismoi (later systematized as the seven deadly sins in Western Christianity): gluttony, lust, avarice, sadness, anger, acedia, vainglory, and pride. Watchfulness (νῆψις) is the practice of observing and refusing entrance to destructive logismoi before they take root in the heart. The goal is not the absence of thought but the guarding of the nous from thoughts that lead the soul away from God and toward the passions.
Magnificat: The hymn of the Virgin Mary recorded in Luke 1:46–55, beginning "My soul magnifies the Lord." A canticle used prominently in Orthodox liturgical worship, celebrating God's saving acts.
Makrothumia: Long-suffering or patient endurance (μακροθυμία), from makros (long) and thumos (passion/anger). In Orthodox ascetic theology, makrothumia is the patient bearing of wrongs, delays, and suffering without retaliation or resentment. It is the virtue that restrains and redirects the passion of anger (θυμός) — not suppressing it but consecrating it to God's timing and justice. Paul lists makrothumia among the fruits of the Spirit (Gal 5:22) and as the first characteristic of love (1 Cor 13:4: "love is patient"). In the ascetic schema, makrothumia belongs to the work of katharsis: the soul that can bear false accusation or unjust treatment without reactive passion is a soul whose anger has been purified and whose peace is rooted in God rather than in external vindication.
Merimna: From Greek merimna (μέριμνα), "anxiety," "care," or "worry" — from merizō (to divide, to be pulled apart). In Orthodox ascetic theology, merimna names the passion of anxious preoccupation — the soul's compulsive calculation about provision, safety, reputation, or outcomes that fragments the nous and prevents it from attending to God. Christ directly addresses merimna in Matthew 6:25-34 ("do not be anxious about your life") and Matthew 10:29-31 (the sparrow passage), grounding the remedy not in psychological technique but in the theological reality of the Father's exhaustive providence. The Fathers, especially Chrysostom, treat merimna as a species of functional atheism: the soul that calculates anxiously reveals that it does not truly believe God's care extends to the details of its life. The ascetic remedy is the Jesus Prayer practiced in the moment of arising anxiety, redirecting the nous from its scattered calculations back to the single object of divine trust. Merimna belongs to the katharsis stage as one of the logismoi that must be identified and refused entrance by the watchful heart.
Manna: Hebrew: mān hū ("What is it?"). The miraculous bread God provided to Israel in the wilderness during the Exodus (Exodus 16). In Orthodox typological interpretation, manna is a type of the Eucharist — the bread from heaven that sustains God's people on their journey toward the Promised Land. Christ Himself interprets the typology in John 6:31–35 and 6:48–51, contrasting the manna that left Israel still mortal with Himself as the true Bread of Life that grants eternal life. The manna stored in the Ark of the Covenant (Hebrews 9:4) further prefigures the Eucharistic Body preserved in the tabernacle of the Church.
Mariology: The theological study of the Virgin Mary — her role, titles, veneration, and significance in salvation history and Church doctrine.
Metanoia: From Greek metanoia (μετάνοια), literally "a change of mind" — but more precisely, in Orthodox usage, the turning of the whole person. The standard English translation "repentance" can mislead because it suggests primarily regret or remorse; metanoia is rather a reorientation of the nous, the heart, and the life away from the passions and toward God. It is not a single act but the continuous interior movement that constitutes the Christian life from baptism to the grave. The Damascus road encounter (Acts 9, 26) is metanoia in its most dramatic form — Saul's entire trajectory turned by the appearance of Christ — but every prayer of the Publican ("God, be merciful to me a sinner"), every confession, every return to the Jesus Prayer after distraction is the same metanoia in miniature. The desert fathers and the Philokalia tradition treat metanoia as inseparable from compunction (katanyxis): the soul pierced by awareness of its distance from God is the soul able to turn. Acts 26:20's pairing of metanoia with "works worthy of repentance" expresses the Orthodox principle of synergeia — true turning produces a transformed life.
Nepsis: From Greek nēphō (νήφω), "to be sober, watchful, alert." The Orthodox ascetic practice of interior watchfulness — the sober, attentive monitoring of thoughts (logismoi) and interior states as they arise, so that the heart may remain clear and oriented toward God. Nepsis is the opposite of spiritual distraction or numbness. The neptic tradition (centered in hesychast asceticism and the Philokalia) teaches that the first movement of prayer is to "guard the mind" — standing at the door of the heart and refusing entrance to distracting or harmful logismoi before they settle. 1 Peter 5:8 ("Be sober, be watchful") is the scriptural anchor for this practice.
Nous: From Greek nous (νοῦς), variously translated as "mind," "intellect," or "spirit." In Orthodox anthropology, the nous is the highest faculty of the human person — the eye of the soul, the capacity for direct perception of God and spiritual realities. It is distinct from the rational mind (διάνοια) that processes discursive thought. In its fallen state, the nous is scattered, distracted, and darkened — drawn to the logismoi and unable to perceive God clearly. The goal of Orthodox ascetic practice is the purification and gathering of the nous, so that it can return to the heart (καρδία) and there be united with God in prayer. Hesychasm is fundamentally the tradition of nous-purification; the Jesus Prayer is its primary instrument.
Orthros: The Morning Office of the Byzantine Rite — the first of the daily liturgical hours, prayed at dawn or before the Divine Liturgy. Orthros corresponds broadly to the Western Matins/Lauds and is the primary structure in which Orthodox Christians formally present the day's first hours to God. It typically includes the Six Psalms (Hexapsalmos), canons, troparia, and concludes with the Great Doxology. As the liturgical embodiment of Romans 6:13 — "present yourselves to God as being alive from the dead" — Orthros shapes the Christian's daily rhythm of self-offering: the day is consecrated before it is consumed. In parish practice, Orthros is usually served before the Sunday Liturgy.
Ousia: From Greek ousia (οὐσία), "essence," "being," or "substance" — the abstract noun of einai, "to be." In Trinitarian theology, ousia names the one divine essence (or nature) shared by the three hypostases (Persons) of the Holy Trinity — the basis of the Nicene confession that the Son is homoousios (consubstantial) with the Father. In the theology of God's knowability, the divine ousia is what God is in Himself: absolutely transcendent, incomprehensible, and unapproachable — no creature can see or participate in it (Exodus 33:20). This is the counterpart of the divine energeia (energies): God is unknowable in essence yet truly known and participated in through His uncreated energies. St. Gregory Palamas pressed this distinction against Barlaam to defend the reality of theosis without collapsing the creature-Creator distinction — were the creature to touch the essence itself, it would "either cease to exist or become God." See [[concept_palamism_and_divine_energies]] and [[concept_theosis]].
Panagia: Greek: Panagia (Παναγία). "All-Holy." The primary Orthodox title for the Virgin Mary, acknowledging her unique holiness as the Theotokos (God-bearer). The term does not claim Mary is divine but honors her as the supremely holy creature — the most perfect human person — in whom humanity's cooperation with divine grace reached its fullest expression. Used throughout Orthodox liturgical hymns, prayers, and iconographic tradition.
Pantheism: The philosophical and theological position that God and the universe are identical — that God is not a personal being distinct from creation but is the totality of all that exists. Orthodox Christianity rejects pantheism as incompatible with the doctrine of creation ex nihilo (out of nothing) and the essential distinction between Creator and creature. Orthodox theology affirms that God is present throughout creation through His energies but remains wholly distinct from it in His essence — a position sometimes called panentheism but distinct from strict pantheism.
Paraclete: From Greek Paraklētos (Παράκλητος), "advocate," "comforter," or "helper" — literally "one called alongside." In the Farewell Discourse (John 14–16), Jesus promises to send "another Paraclete" — the Holy Spirit — after His departure, implying that Christ Himself is the first Paraclete (cf. 1 John 2:1, where He is the disciples' "advocate" with the Father). Orthodox theology reads the Paraclete not as a replacement for the absent Christ but as the mode of His continued indwelling in the Church — the theological ground of the hesychast life of prayer. The Paraclete "will not speak on His own authority" (John 16:13) but glorifies the Son, extending the perichoretic life of Father and Son into the soul through theosis. The word paraklesis (comfort, encouragement) in Acts 20:12 echoes the same root, linking apostolic consolation to the Spirit's own consoling presence.
Parrhesia: From Greek parrēsia (παρρησία), "boldness," "free speech," or "confidence before God." In the New Testament and patristic tradition, parrhesia describes the bold, unashamed confession of Christ — the interior freedom that speaks openly about faith without calculating social cost. It is the opposite of the whispered, fear-governed speech of those who know the truth but suppress it (cf. John 7:13). In Orthodox ascetic theology, parrhesia is a fruit of katharsis and photismos: the soul purified of the passion of cowardice (δειλία) and illumined by the Spirit gains the freedom to confess Christ openly regardless of consequence. The Jesus Prayer practiced in the heart is itself a daily exercise of parrhesia — a direct, unguarded address to Christ.
Parthenos: Greek: parthenos. "Virgin." Used in Orthodox theology especially of the Ever-Virgin Mary (Aeiparthenos). Also the term used in the Septuagint translation of Isaiah 7:14.
Penthos: From Greek penthos (πένθος), "mourning," "grief," or "lamentation." In Orthodox ascetic theology, penthos is the holy sorrow that arises from genuine encounter with one's own spiritual condition before God — the grief of a soul that perceives its distance from the divine life it was made for and aches to return. Closely related to compunction (katanyxis), penthos is its affective and sustained dimension: where compunction names the sudden piercing awareness, penthos names the mourning that follows. The Beatitude "Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted" (Matt 5:4) is read by the Fathers (especially St. John Climacus and St. Isaac the Syrian) as the blessing of penthos — the souls who grieve their condition receive not worldly comfort but divine consolation. Penthos is not depression or despair but a purifying grief that opens the heart to God's mercy; it belongs to the katharsis stage and is the interior condition out of which genuine prayer grows.
Perilypos: From Greek perilypos (περίλυπος) — "encompassed with grief," "profoundly sorrowful" (from peri- "around, completely" + lypē "grief, sorrow"). The word used in Matthew 26:38 when Christ says "My soul is very sorrowful, even to death" (perilypos estin hē psychē mou heōs thanatou). The compound prefix peri- intensifies the noun: not merely sadness but grief so total it envelops the soul as the sea surrounds a drowning person. The phrase "even to death" (heōs thanatou) indicates grief at the very threshold of death — a sorrow so deep it approaches the point where the soul would expire from it. Chrysostom and other Fathers cite this verse as one of the strongest demonstrations of Christ's genuine human emotional life, and therefore of the Incarnation against Docetism. The term distinguishes the intensity of Christ's anticipatory anguish in Gethsemane from ordinary sadness or theological resignation.
Perichoresis: From Greek perichōrēsis (περιχώρησις), "mutual indwelling" or "interpenetration" (from peri- "around" + chōrein "to make room for, to contain"). The technical Orthodox term for the mutual indwelling of the three Persons of the Holy Trinity: the Father is in the Son, the Son is in the Father, and the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father and rests in the Son — three Persons sharing one undivided divine life without confusion of Persons or division of essence. The scriptural anchor is Christ's words in John 14:10-11 and 17:21 ("I am in the Father, and the Father in Me"). The doctrine was developed by St. John of Damascus and the Cappadocians to safeguard both the unity of God and the distinction of Persons against modalism on one side and tritheism on the other. Perichoresis is also extended analogically to Christology (the mutual indwelling of the divine and human natures in the one Person of Christ) and to ecclesiology (the mutual indwelling of the faithful in Christ and in one another — John 17:21–23), making it the structural grammar of theosis: union with God is a perichoretic participation, not a fusion.
Philokalia: Greek: Philokalia (Φιλοκαλία), "love of the beautiful" or "love of the good" (from philos, love + kalos, beautiful, good). The great anthology of Orthodox ascetic and mystical texts compiled by St. Nikodemos of the Holy Mountain and St. Makarios of Corinth, first published in Venice in 1782. The Philokalia gathers writings of Desert Fathers, hesychast masters, and spiritual theologians from the 4th through 15th centuries, centered on hesychia (stillness), nepsis (watchfulness), and the guarding and purification of the nous through unceasing prayer. It is the primary sourcebook of the Orthodox hesychast tradition and indispensable for understanding patristic spiritual theology. The Slavonic translation (Dobrotolubiye) by St. Paisios Velichkovsky (1793) and its Russian version sparked a wide revival of the Jesus Prayer in the 19th century.
Phobos: Greek: phobos (φόβος), "fear" or "awe." In Orthodox theology, the fear of the Lord is not primarily servile terror (the fear of punishment) but the reverential awe that arises from genuine perception of God's holiness, majesty, and love. The Fathers distinguish between a lower stage — fear of punishment that restrains the passions — and the higher stage of phobos born of love: the soul's dread of separation from, or of grieving, the One it loves above all else. Psalm 33 (LXX) makes the fear of the Lord the explicit subject of its catechesis (v. 11: "I will teach you the fear of the Lord"), treating it as the foundational disposition from which all virtue flows. In the sapiential tradition, "the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 9:10 LXX); in the ascetic tradition, it is the soil in which nepsis, compunction, and humility grow.
Photismos: From Greek phōtismos (φωτισμός), "illumination" (from phōs, "light"). The second stage of the threefold spiritual journey in Orthodox theology: katharsis → photismos → theosis. The soul purified of its dominant passions begins to receive the illumination of the Holy Spirit — a direct experience of divine grace that clarifies the nous and enables prayer of the heart. Photismos is not intellectual insight but the participation of the purified nous in divine light. The term connects to the ancient name for baptism (phōtismos — "illumination"), suggesting that the spiritual journey recapitulates and deepens the grace first given at the font.
Phronema: From Greek phronēma (φρόνημα), "mind," "disposition," or "mentality." In Orthodox usage, the phronema of the Church refers to the characteristic mind, sensibility, and way of perceiving reality that is formed in a person through full immersion in the Church's liturgical, doctrinal, and ascetic life. It is the "mind of the Church" — not merely intellectual assent to doctrines, but the transformation of how one sees, feels, and responds, shaped by the Fathers, the Liturgy, the Scriptures, and the sacraments. Developing the Orthodox phronema is the long work of catechesis and formation; the catechumen is only beginning to acquire it.
Praxis: From Greek praxis (πρᾶξις), "action," "practice," or "doing." In Orthodox ascetic theology, praxis refers to the active life of virtue and ascetic struggle — the practical dimension of the spiritual life involving fasting, prayer, almsgiving, obedience, and the disciplined engagement with the passions. Praxis is distinguished from theoria (contemplation) as the necessary foundation: one cannot arrive at the direct perception of God without first doing the work of purification. The two are not opposed but sequential and complementary. In the three-stage framework, praxis belongs primarily to the katharsis stage, though it continues throughout the spiritual life.
Prautes: From Greek praütēs (πραΰτης), "meekness," "gentleness," or "mildness." In Orthodox ascetic theology, prautes is the virtue that results from the healing of the passion of anger (thumos): not the suppression of the soul's irascible power, but its right ordering — the energy of anger converted from self-defense and reactivity into patient endurance and compassion for others. Gregory of Nyssa, in his Homilies on the Beatitudes, treats "Blessed are the meek" as the stage of the soul's ascent in which the logismoi of anger and pride have been sufficiently tamed that the soul no longer expends its energy on self-assertion. Prautes is closely related to tapeinosis (humility) but is specifically the affective fruit of that humility when tested by mistreatment or opposition. In the desert tradition, abba Moses taught that the monk who has not yet acquired prautes cannot safely receive spiritual knowledge, because anger will distort it.
Prayer of the Heart: The interior goal of the Jesus Prayer in the Orthodox hesychast tradition: the state in which the prayer "prays itself," having descended from the lips (vocal prayer) to the mind (mental attention) to the kardia (the spiritual heart), so that it becomes continuous and effortless — "no longer something you do… something you are." St. John Climacus expresses its aim: "Let the remembrance of Jesus be united with your every breath." It is the union of the nous (the soul's perceptive faculty) with the heart, where, freed from the scattering of the logismoi, the person prays unceasingly (1 Thessalonians 5:17) and may receive the illumination of the uncreated light. The prayer of the heart is the heart of hesychasm and is pursued under spiritual guidance to avoid prelest; it belongs not to monastics alone but is the vocation of every baptized Christian. See: [[Komboskini]], Hesychia, Nepsis, Photismos.
Prelest: From Church Slavonic prelest (прелесть), "delusion," "deception," or "enchantment." In Orthodox ascetic theology, prelest is the state of spiritual self-deception — the condition of mistaking self-generated or demonically-inflated interior states for genuine grace. The term corresponds to the Greek plánē (πλάνη), "wandering" or "error." Prelest is the primary danger of the solitary and advanced spiritual life, and the Fathers warn against it with particular urgency for beginners who attempt contemplative prayer without a spiritual guide. It typically presents as an excess of spiritual confidence — a conviction that one's own experiences, insights, or zeal are reliable guides to truth — without the corrective of humility and submission to the apostolic tradition. Sts. Ignatius Brianchaninov and Theophan the Recluse both teach that spontaneous prayer, emotional spiritual experiences, and self-assured theological certainty are the primary gateways to prelest for those not yet purified through katharsis. The remedy is radical humility (tapeinosis), submission to a spiritual father or the Church's tradition, and fidelity to the fixed prayers of the Church rather than improvised spontaneous prayer. Apollos (Acts 18:22-28) models the opposite of prelest: genuine zeal and scriptural knowledge received humbly from those who carry the fuller apostolic deposit.
Proskynesis: From Greek proskunēsis (προσκύνησις), "prostration" or "reverence." The physical act of bowing down or falling prostrate before the holy — before God, before icons, or as a gesture of deep reverence to a holy person. In Orthodox worship, prostrations (full or partial) are regular postures of prayer, particularly during Great Lent and at certain moments in the Divine Liturgy. Ascetically, proskynesis denotes the interior disposition of the soul stripped of pride that instinctively bows before the holy — the posture Cornelius exemplifies in Acts 10:25. Peter's correction ("Stand up, I am only a man") illustrates the proper ordering: reverential proskynesis toward holy persons, but worship (latria) reserved for God alone. The Seventh Ecumenical Council (Nicaea II, 787) defined the veneration of icons as a different order of proskynesis from the worship due to God.
Stenagmos: From Greek stenagmos (στεναγμός), "groaning" or "deep sighing" — from the verb stenazō (στενάζω), "to groan, to sigh." A stenagmos is the wordless, involuntary moan forced out of a person by interior anguish — prayer beneath the level of articulate speech, the sound the soul makes when its pain has outrun its vocabulary. In Orthodox theology the word carries a rich biblical theology of groaning that the LXX makes audible: God "heard the groaning of the children of Israel" in Egypt (Exodus 2:24); the penitent of Psalm 37 (LXX) cries "I roared from the groaning of my heart" and is consoled that "my groaning is not hidden from You" (vv. 9–10); and St. Paul uses the same word when "we ourselves groan within ourselves" (Romans 8:23) and when the Holy Spirit intercedes "with groanings that cannot be uttered" (stenagmois alalētois, Romans 8:26). The Fathers thus read groaning not as failed prayer but as prayer in its rawest and most trusting form — the soul's compunction (katanyxis) too deep for words, received in full by the God from whom nothing is hidden. Groaning belongs to the katharsis stage of the spiritual life and is closely allied with penthos (mourning) and the prayer of the heart.
Synergeia: Greek: synergeia. "Cooperation" or "working together." In Orthodox theology, the cooperation between divine grace and human free will in the process of salvation. Distinct from both Pelagian self-effort and Reformed monergism.
Synkatabasis: From Greek synkatabasis (συγκατάβασις), "condescension" or "accommodation" — literally "a coming down with." A key term in St. John Chrysostom's theology, naming the divine act by which God accommodates Himself to human limitation and condition. Chrysostom uses it to describe both Scripture (God speaking in human language at the level humanity can receive) and the Incarnation (God taking on flesh so that the creature's encounter with God is possible). In the ascetic tradition, synkatabasis extends to the entire economy of salvation: Christ meeting the disciples at the lakeshore, cooking their breakfast, speaking in parables, washing feet — each is a movement of divine condescension in which infinite majesty veils itself in approachable simplicity. The proper human response to synkatabasis is not familiarity but awe-filled gratitude: the recognition that God's tenderness toward us is not weakness but love bearing us where we are.
Tapeinosis: Greek: tapeinōsis (ταπείνωσις), "lowliness," "abasement," "humility." In Orthodox ascetic theology, tapeinosis names the interior disposition of genuine self-knowledge before God — the soul's accurate perception of its own poverty and dependence. It is not self-deprecation but truth: the recognition that all virtue, all prayer, all good is gift. The Fathers regard tapeinosis as the foundation of the spiritual life, because pride (the first passion) is dismantled precisely by the soul's honest acknowledgment of its smallness. St. John Climacus places humility at the summit of The Ladder of Divine Ascent, not merely its beginning. Tapeinosis is cultivated in katharsis and perfected in the later stages of the spiritual life, where the soul learns that its "poverty" is the very vessel God fills.
Telos: Greek: telos. "End," "goal," or "purpose." The final cause or ultimate aim toward which something naturally strives. In Orthodox anthropology, the telos of the human person is theosis — union with God.
Theologia: From Greek theologia (θεολογία) — but in its precise patristic and Evagrian sense, distinct from "theology" as academic study. For Evagrius of Pontus and the hesychast tradition, theologia names the highest state of the nous: the soul's direct, wordless standing before God — not thinking about God but attending to Him. It is the fruit of theoria and the threshold of theosis, the place the spiritual life is moving toward when praxis and katharsis have done their work. St. Evagrius: "A theologian is one who prays truly; one who prays truly is a theologian." This is why the Church venerates St. John the Theologian (the Evangelist), St. Gregory the Theologian (Nazianzus), and St. Symeon the New Theologian — not for their academic achievements but for the depth of their prayer and direct encounter with the divine. Theologia in this sense stands in contrast to theologoumena (theological propositions and opinions) and oikonomia (the theology of God's saving acts in history): it is the goal those disciplines are ordered toward, not a more advanced form of study.
Theologoumena: From Greek theologoumena (θεολογούμενα), plural of theologoumenon — "theological opinions" or "things theologized about." In Orthodox usage, theologoumena are theological positions held by respected Fathers or theologians that are not formally defined as dogma by an Ecumenical Council. They are authoritative opinions, not binding definitions: a catechumen may encounter them, weigh them, and hold them with the respect due to the source, but they are not articles of faith in the same sense as the Nicene Creed. The distinction matters pastorally: not everything a Father wrote is conciliar dogma, and Orthodox theology is not reducible to the private opinions of even the greatest teachers. In the ascetic tradition, theologoumena is sometimes used in contrast to theologia (direct encounter with God): propositions and formulations are necessary and good, but they exist to carry the soul toward the encounter, not to substitute for it.
Theotokos: From Greek Theotokos (Θεοτόκος), "God-bearer" or "one who gave birth to God" — from theos (God) + tiktō (to give birth). The primary Christological title given to the Virgin Mary by the Third Ecumenical Council (Ephesus, 431 AD), affirming that the child she bore is truly the eternal divine Logos — not a human person to whom the divine Logos was later joined. The title is therefore first a Christological claim: if Christ is truly one Person with two natures (divine and human), then the one who bore him is the one who bore God himself. Nestorius proposed "Christotokos" (Christ-bearer) instead; Ephesus condemned this as inadequate, because it implied that Mary bore a merely human person who was later joined to the divine. For Orthodox theology, the Theotokos is not merely the biological mother of the human Jesus — she is the one through whose free cooperation (fiat) the eternal Son entered the world. This makes her the supreme model of human cooperation with divine grace and the one who stands at the hinge of the divine economy. Within divine council theology, the Theotokos occupies the role of the gebirah (queen mother) in the Davidic court: Psalm 45 (LXX), cited in Hebrews as Messianic, depicts a queen at the king's right hand — and if Christ is the Davidic king, the highest place in his divine council belongs to his mother. Her intercessory role flows from this structural position, not from any divine attribute of her own. She is a creature — the highest and most fully glorified creature — whose prayers are supremely effective because she stands nearest to the throne.
Theophany: From Greek theophaneia (θεοφάνεια), "appearance of God" (theos, God + phainein, to appear). A visible manifestation of God to human beings in history. Old Testament theophanies include the burning bush (Exodus 3), the pillar of cloud and fire, the giving of the Law on Sinai, and Isaiah's temple vision (Isaiah 6). Orthodox theology reads these appearances as manifestations of the pre-incarnate Logos — not the Father directly, who remains invisible in essence. The Transfiguration on Tabor is the supreme New Testament theophany: the disciples behold the uncreated divine light in the incarnate Son. In the Orthodox liturgical calendar, Theophany (January 6 / Τα Θεοφάνεια) is the feast of Christ's Baptism in the Jordan — the moment the Holy Trinity is revealed together: the Son in the water, the Father's voice from heaven, and the Spirit descending as a dove. It is one of the twelve Great Feasts.
Theoria: From Greek theōria (θεωρία), "vision," "contemplation," "beholding." In Orthodox spiritual theology, theoria is the direct, experiential perception of God — not through concepts or images but through the purified nous's participation in the divine uncreated light. It corresponds to the advanced stages of the spiritual life (photismos and theosis), and is distinguished from discursive reasoning (dianoia) or speculative theology. Paired with praxis (ascetic action), theoria names the contemplative dimension toward which the active life is ordered: the soul that has done the work of katharsis begins to see — first the illumination of divine grace, then progressively the uncreated light itself (the experience of the Transfiguration that the saints share). Theoria is not reserved for monastics but is the universal telos of the baptized person; Psalm 25:14 ("the secret of the Lord is with those who fear Him") names it scripturally as the intimate covenant knowledge given to the humble. The vision given to the pure of heart (Matthew 5:8) is theoria in its New Testament form.
Theosis: From Greek thēōsis (θέωσις), "deification" or "divinization." The central goal of Christian life in Orthodox theology — the process by which the human person is united with God and genuinely participates in the divine nature (2 Peter 1:4) without loss of creaturely identity. Theosis is not the absorption of the self into God (which would be pantheism) but authentic communion with Him through the divine energies (see: Palamism). St. Athanasius provides the formula: "God became man that man might become god." Theosis unfolds through the three stages of the spiritual life (katharsis → photismos → theosis) and is mediated through the sacraments, Scripture, ascetic struggle, and prayer. It is not reserved for monastics but is the vocation of every baptized Christian.
Toll Houses: From the Greek telōnia (τελώνια), "customs posts" or "toll stations." A vivid image found in some Orthodox ascetic and hagiographical writings (most famously the vision of the nun Theodora in the Life of St. Basil the Younger) depicting the soul's ascent after death past a series of stations where demons accuse it of particular sins — pride, anger, greed, lust, and so on — while angels defend it and its repentance answers the charges. The toll houses are not dogma: they are a theologoumenon (a permitted theological opinion), debated among the Orthodox to this day, and best received "not as a map of the air, but as a warning about the heart" — an externalized picture of the war against the logismoi and passions that is waged in this life. The right posture is the Church's own: neither dismiss the imagery nor dogmatize it, but let it sharpen the examination of conscience and drive the soul to confession, where each accusation is answered in advance.
Trisagion: From Greek Trisagion (Τρισάγιον), "thrice-holy" (from tris — three, + hagios — holy). The ancient liturgical hymn sung throughout Orthodox worship: "Holy God, Holy Mighty, Holy Immortal, have mercy on us" — repeated three times. Its origins are traced to a fifth-century vision in Constantinople. The Trisagion appears at multiple points in Orthodox services: the Divine Liturgy, the Daily Office, and at the beginning of most prayer rules. Each repetition addresses a different Person of the Trinity — Holy God (the Father), Holy Mighty (the Son, whose power was revealed in the Resurrection), Holy Immortal (the Spirit). The Trisagion is thus a Trinitarian confession enacted through rhythm and repetition, training the praying person to address the Holy Trinity as a single divine reality in three distinct Persons. The hymn echoes the seraphic song of Isaiah 6:3 ("Holy, holy, holy, Lord of Sabaoth") and connects the Church's earthly worship to the unceasing doxology of the heavenly court.
Thymos: From Greek thymos (θυμός), variously translated as "spiritedness," "passionate drive," or "wrath." In Orthodox ascetic theology, thymos is the irascible faculty of the soul — the capacity for anger or aggressive energy. In its ordered state, thymos is the soul's righteous energy directed against sin, temptation, and injustice. In its disordered form (what the tradition calls the passion of wrath), thymos becomes unbridled anger directed at persons rather than at evil — one of the eight primary passions catalogued by Evagrius of Pontus and John Cassian. The Fathers, especially Chrysostom and Dorotheos of Gaza, distinguish between righteous anger (ὀργή) and the passion of thymos: the former is brief, targeted at injustice, and yields to reason; the latter is a disordered, self-protective surge that clouds the nous and generates sin against neighbor. Paul's sharp rebuke of Ananias (Acts 23:3) followed by immediate self-correction illustrates the distinction: what might appear as thymos is instantly governed by diakrisis, returning the soul to its formed conscience.
Thumikon: From Greek thumikon (θυμικόν), "the irascible faculty" or "the spirited part" — from thymos (θυμός, passion, drive, anger). In the patristic tripartite anthropology of the soul, the thumikon is the soul's capacity for energy, assertiveness, and combat. In its ordered state, it is the warrior faculty: directed against sin, temptation, and the logismoi, it provides the energy for ascetic struggle and righteous action. In its disordered state, it fires against human persons rather than against evil, becoming the passion of anger that Matthew 5:22 exposes as the interior form of murder. Evagrius of Pontus and John Climacus both teach that the conversion of the thumikon — from person-directed anger to sin-directed energy — is one of the defining works of katharsis. The full tripartite faculty model: logistikon (rational faculty / nous), thumikon (irascible), epithumētikon (desiderative). See also: thymos, prautes (the virtue that heals the disordered thumikon).
Vainglory: From Greek kenodoxia (κενοδοξία), "empty glory" or "hollow reputation." One of the eight primary passions in Orthodox ascetic theology (following Evagrius of Pontus and St. John Cassian). Vainglory is the compulsive need for human approval, recognition, or the appearance of virtue — the seeking of one's glory from other people rather than from God. It operates subtly, often attaching itself to genuinely good acts: one fasts, but wants to be seen fasting; one prays, but performs piety for observers. Vainglory is considered particularly dangerous because it imitates virtue while corrupting it from within. The remedy is humility (tapeinophrosynē) and the practice of hidden good works directed entirely toward the Father.
Xerasia: From Greek ξηρασία (dryness). In Orthodox ascetic and mystical theology, the experience of spiritual dryness — a season in which prayer feels mechanical, God seems absent or distant, and the affective supports for devotion have collapsed. Xerasia is not a sign of God's absence but a common stage of purification: the soul is being weaned from consolation-seeking and trained to pray from naked will and trust rather than from emotional warmth. The desert and hesychast traditions consistently prescribe the same remedy: maintain your rule of prayer without modification, without seeking the return of feeling, and without interpreting dryness as punishment. Xerasia belongs to the stage of katharsis and is the condition in which genuine hypomonē is formed.
